title: Cornmeal exfoliating cold-process soap
subtitle: A gently abrasive bar with fine cornmeal stirred in at trace, built on an olive, coconut, and shea base
excerpt: Fine cornmeal folded into a soap batter at trace gives this bar a soft, scrubby texture that polishes skin without scratching. The oil base of olive, coconut, and shea produces a conditioning bar with good lather. Cornmeal softens in the bar over the cure period and provides a mild exfoliation on use.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, shea butter 0.128. 5% superfat; lye discount 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), chapter on natural additives and exfoliants. Fine cornmeal as a soap exfoliant: Bramble Berry natural ingredients guide (2023). Cornmeal softens over cure time and offers a milder abrasion than oat bran or ground walnut shell.

recipe.batchNotes: Always recalculate lye weight when changing oil amounts. The cornmeal is an additive, not an oil; it does not affect the lye calculation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 2 days in the mould before unmoulding. A full 4-week cure produces the best lather; 6 weeks gives a harder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Discard any bar that develops orange spots (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5% superfat, 5% of the oil weight stays as free oil in the finished bar, giving a mild, conditioning lather.
The point at which the soap batter thickens enough that a drizzle on the surface holds its shape briefly. The moment to add exfoliants or other late-stage additives.
The chemical reaction between fat and lye that produces soap and glycerine. SAP values vary by oil; the lye weight in this recipe is calculated for the specific oil blend and superfat.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out after 2 days. Cut into bars. Wear protective gloves; the bars remain caustic until fully cured.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the inside of the forearm. The bar is ready to use. Cornmeal will have softened to a gentle scrub texture.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Fully cured and hard. The exfoliating texture is at its best at 6 weeks.

content.text: Fine cornmeal stirred into soap batter at
trace
creates a bar with a light scrubby texture that polishes rather than scratches. The oil base of 50% olive, 30% coconut, and 20% shea gives good lather and a conditioning bar. At 5%
superfat

and 33% lye discount, the bar is mild enough for daily use on normal skin. Cornmeal softens during the cure period so the texture on use is gentler than the raw meal suggests. Yield is 8 bars of around 115 g each.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before starting. Lye handling comes first.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt if solid
20% of oil weight; melt together with coconut oil
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat
Fine-ground; added at trace. Medium or coarse meal is too scratchy.

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a well-ventilated space with goggles and protective gloves on. Weigh the sodium hydroxide into a small container. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream, stirring continuously. Never add water to lye. The solution heats quickly and gives off steam. Set aside to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the oils

Melt the coconut oil and shea butter together on low heat until just liquid. Weigh into a heat-safe bowl and add the olive oil. Cool to 38 to 42°C. The lye solution and oil blend should be at similar temperatures before combining.
Blend to trace and add cornmeal

Pour the lye solution into the oils in a steady stream. Use the stick blender in short bursts, stirring between each, until the batter reaches light trace. Stir in the fine cornmeal quickly and blend for 5 seconds to distribute evenly. Pour immediately into the mould, as the cornmeal can thicken the batter further.
Insulate and cure
Wrap the mould in a towel. Leave for 2 days before unmoulding. The
saponification

reaction completes over this period. Cut the loaf into bars, wearing protective gloves. Arrange on a rack with space between each bar. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dry spot out of direct sunlight, turning every two weeks.
Storage and shelf life
Cured bars keep two years on an open rack in a cool, dark place. Avoid sealed containers, which trap moisture. Discard any bar that develops orange spots (DOS) or a paint-like smell.
